1: śāmaḥtî bĕʾōmĕrîm lî bêt yhwh nēlēk — "I rejoiced when they said to me, 'Let us go to the house of the LORD!'" — the verb śāmaḥtî (I rejoiced, Qal perfect) is inceptive: "I broke into joy." The invitation from the plural ("they said to me") is the first moment of the pilgrimage experience — the community calling the individual into the journey. bêt yhwh (house of the LORD) — the temple, YHWH's royal dwelling on Zion. The superscription names David as author, placing this joy in the context of David's longing to build the temple (2 Sam 7).
2: ʿōmĕdôt hāyû raglênû bišʿārayik yĕrûšālāim — "our feet are standing within your gates, O Jerusalem!" — the perfect tense hāyû (have been/are) with the present participle ʿōmĕdôt (standing) creates a vivid present: the feet that were journeying now stand inside the city. The direct address to Jerusalem ("your gates") personifies the city. The gates (šĕʿārîm) were the legal, commercial, and social hub of ancient cities — the pilgrim's feet standing in the gates signal full arrival.
3: yĕrûšālayim habbĕnûyāh kĕʿîr šeḥubbĕrāh-llāh yaḥdāw — "Jerusalem — built as a city that is closely joined/compact together" — šeḥubbĕrāh-llāh yaḥdāw (that is joined together as one) — the Pual participle of ḥābar (to join, couple, unite). The architectural compactness of Jerusalem — its dense stone buildings and close-packed topography — becomes a metaphor for the unity of the covenant people who gather there. The phrase may also reflect the joining of the two kingdoms (Judah and Israel) in pilgrimage to the shared sanctuary.
4: šeššām ʿālû šĕbāṭîm šibṭê-yāh ʿēdût lĕyiśrāʾēl lĕhôdôt lĕšēm yhwh — "to which the tribes go up — the tribes of the LORD — as a testimony for Israel, to give thanks to the name of the LORD" — šibṭê-yāh (tribes of the LORD, with the divine shortform Yah) — the tribes belong to YHWH, not merely to their geographical territories. ʿēdût (testimony, statute) — the command to appear before the LORD at the festivals (Exodus 23:17; Psalms 34:23) is here called a testimony/statute. lĕhôdôt (to give thanks/praise, Hiphil infinitive of yādāh) — the purpose of the pilgrimage is thanksgiving.
5: kî-šāmmāh yāšĕbû kissĕʾôt lĕmišpāṭ kissĕʾôt lĕbêt dāwid — "for there the thrones of judgment stand — the thrones belonging to the house of David" — kissĕʾôt lĕmišpāṭ (thrones for judgment) — the judicial seat of the Davidic king was in Jerusalem; the pilgrimage center was also the royal court for legal appeals (cf. 2 Samuel 15:1-6; 1 Kings 7:7). The plural kissĕʾôt (thrones) may refer to multiple courtrooms or the dynasty's collective judicial presence. The Davidic kingship and the temple worship are here inseparably linked.
6: šaʾălû šĕlôm yĕrûšālāim yišlāyû ʾōhăbāyik — "pray for the peace of Jerusalem! May all who love this city flourish" — the triple wordplay: šaʾălû (ask/pray) + šĕlôm (peace/shalom) + yĕrûšālāim (Jerusalem) — the city's very name may be heard as yĕrû-šālēm (city of peace), making the command to pray for its peace a play on its identity. yišlāyû (may they be at ease, prosper, have security, from šālāh, to be at ease) — those who love Jerusalem will share in the shalom they pray for.
7: yĕhî-šālôm bĕḥêlēk šalwāh bĕʾarmĕnôtāyik — "may there be peace within your walls and safety within your strongholds/citadels" — ḥêlēk (your walls, ramparts, from ḥēl = outer wall or moat) — the first line of the city's defense. šalwāh (safety, ease, prosperity) — a near synonym of šālôm, continuing the sound-cluster. ʾarmĕnôtāyik (your palaces, citadels, towers) — the innermost defended structures. The movement from outer walls to inner palaces maps the prayer's scope from the perimeter to the heart of the city.
8: lĕmaʿan ʾaḥay wĕrēʿāy ʾădabbĕrāh-nnāʾ šālôm bāk — "for the sake of my brothers and friends I now say, 'Peace be with you!'" — the psalmist's motivation is relational: the prayer for peace is not nationalist sentiment but affection for the ʾaḥim (brothers) and rēʿîm (friends/companions) who share the pilgrimage. The address bāk (in/with you) is to Jerusalem, confirming the personification of the city throughout the psalm.
9: lĕmaʿan bêt-yhwh ʾĕlōhênû ʾăbaqqĕšāh ṭôb lāk — "for the sake of the house of the LORD our God I will work for your wellbeing" — the final motivation: the welfare of Jerusalem is sought because YHWH's house is there. ʾăbaqqĕšāh ṭôb (I will seek good) — active pursuit, not passive wishing. The psalm ends with the divine presence (the house of the LORD) as the ultimate ground for all the peace-praying that precedes it. Jerusalem matters because YHWH has chosen it as his dwelling.
